one_line_takeaway: TriageFuzz uses a white-box surrogate model's attention patterns and refusal-tendency probe to localize and prioritize refusal-sensitive prompt regions and near-miss candidates, cutting black-box jailbreak query costs by over 70% versus PAIR/TAP/GPTFuzz/GCG/AutoDAN while retaining comparable or higher ASR on 6 open-source models and 3 commercial APIs (GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-Sonnet), and remaining substantially more effective than baselines even under standalone and hybrid input-filtering/randomized-smoothing defenses.
key_finding: Across all six open-source target models, TriageFuzz achieves the highest normalized AUC of the ASR-budget curve, exceeding the best baseline by more than 0.20 (20 percentage points). It reaches 90% ASR with over 70% fewer queries than baselines overall; concretely on Gemma-7B it hits ASR=0.9 at a budget of 18 queries versus 62 for the best baseline (71.0% query reduction). In the low-budget regime (Table 1), at budget=10 TriageFuzz attains e.g. 70.5% ASR on Gemma-7B (vs. TAP 45.5%, PAIR 46.0%, GPTFuzz 34.0%, AutoDAN 29.0%, GCG 0.0%) and 42.5% on LLaMA3-8B (vs. TAP 28.5%, PAIR 15.0%, GPTFuzz 1.0%, AutoDAN/GCG 0.0%); at budget=25 it reaches 96.0% (Gemma-7B), 93.5% (Gemma2-9B), 88.0% (LLaMA3-8B), 88.0% (LLaMA3.2-3B), 94.5% (Qwen2.5-7B), 93.0% (Qwen2.5-3B). On commercial APIs (Table 2) at budget=25, TriageFuzz attains 94.0% ASR on GPT-3.5-Turbo (vs. PAIR 74.0%, GPTFuzz 52.0%, TAP 58.0%), 84.0% on GPT-4o (vs. PAIR 61.5%, GPTFuzz 5.0%, TAP 52.0%), and 80.5% on Claude-3.5-Sonnet (vs. PAIR 28.0%, GPTFuzz 3.0%, TAP 24.5%); at budget=10 it already reaches 68.0% (GPT-3.5), 50.0% (GPT-4o), 47.5% (Claude-3.5-Sonnet). Ablation (RQ2, Fig. 8) shows the token-aware design over a 'Uniform' random-mutation variant adds +8 to +32 ASR percentage points depending on model and budget (e.g. LLaMA3-8B: +8 at budget 10 up to +32 at budget 25). Robustness (RQ3): swapping surrogate models changes ASR@25 by only 2.0-3.0 percentage points across target models; swapping attack models changes it by 2.0-4.5 points. Under standalone defenses: PPL filter causes <3 percentage-point ASR degradation; SmoothLLM (Table 3) reduces average ASR across budgets 10-25 substantially per model (e.g. Qwen2.5-7B from 87.9% to 62.4% average) but the defended ASR still exceeds undefended baseline ASRs; LLaMA Guard mitigates but does not neutralize the attack (qualitative, no aggregate number given in the extracted pages). Under hybrid defenses (PPL+SmoothLLM, LLaMA Guard+SmoothLLM), TriageFuzz still outperforms all black-box baselines by a substantial margin, though absolute ASR drops for all methods.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is TriageFuzz, a token-aware, query-efficient black-box jailbreak fuzzing framework for LLMs. It replaces uniform (global) prompt mutation used by prior fuzzing attacks with a three-step evolutionary loop: (1) Token Importance Estimation -- a fixed white-box surrogate model's single 'refusal-critical' attention head (l†, h†), chosen offline by ablating candidate heads and measuring cosine deviation of the SVD-derived 'refusal signature' u1, is used to extract per-token attention weights from the final token as a refusal-importance score s_token(x); (2) Region-Focused Mutation -- an Attacker Model (Vicuna-13B) aggregates fragmented high-score subword tokens into 1-3 contiguous 'trigger spans' via a structured prompt and applies targeted semantic rewrites (obfuscation, scenario injection) only to those spans, preserving task intent; (3) Refusal-Guided Evolution -- a logistic-regression probe on the surrogate's 'reference layer' l* produces a continuous refusal-tendency score s_prompt(x), which is converted via a softmax over negative scores into per-candidate mutation-budget allocations B_i = Round(B*w_i), concentrating the fixed query budget on near-boundary 'near-miss' candidates. The paper motivates this design with two original empirical findings (Section 3): Refusal Tendency Alignment -- pairwise Spearman correlation of refusal-tendency scores across six open-source LLMs is highest at each model's own reference layer l* (avg. ~0.85-0.90+) versus first layer (avg. ~0.60) or final layer (avg. ~0.85, still below reference-layer); and Refusal Semantics Alignment -- cross-model cosine similarity of the SVD-derived refusal signature u1 is consistently high (mostly 0.8-0.98) across six model pairs, suggesting a white-box surrogate's internal signals transfer to unseen black-box targets.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 2.3. Target LLM: closed-source, interactive, rate-limited; only final natural-language outputs are observable, internals hidden. Attacker objective: bypass safety/moderation to elicit harmful/prohibited content in a strict black-box setting, minimizing queries while maximizing ASR. Attacker capabilities: black-box query access only to the target (no gradients, parameters, activations, or training data); the attacker may additionally use an auxiliary open-source 'surrogate' LLM that is architecturally and parametrically independent of the target, extracting refusal-related internal signals from that surrogate (via white-box access to it) to guide black-box attacks on the target. This is a fully in-scope adversarial-ML/jailbreak threat model.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section 7): (1) attack success is judged solely via the automatic MD-Judge classifier rather than large-scale human annotation, though the authors argue their core claims rest on relative comparisons under a unified protocol across all methods, which remain robust even if absolute MD-Judge scores are imperfect; (2) unlike purely query-based baselines, TriageFuzz requires a local white-box surrogate model, incurring additional local compute overhead, which the authors justify as an acceptable tradeoff for red-teaming/adversarial-evaluation use cases focused on exposing worst-case vulnerabilities rather than minimizing local compute. Not stated as a limitation but notable from the text: the refusal-critical head (l†,h†) is selected offline once per surrogate and held fixed, and generalization of this offline-selected head/reference-layer approach was only validated on six open, mostly mid-sized (3B-9B) instruction-tuned model families plus three commercial APIs -- no discussion of scaling to much larger frontier models or of failure cases where cross-model refusal-semantics alignment breaks down (e.g., the anomalously low first-layer/embedding-stage or outlier pairwise correlations visible in the Figure 2/3 heatmaps, such as the 0.30-0.45 range for some Gemma-7B pairs).

datasets_used: HarmBench (malicious prompts across 6 categories: chemical/biological hazards, illegal activity, misinformation/disinformation, harmful content, harassment/bullying, cybercrime)
AdvBench (used in the exploratory study to construct the refusal-inducing set D_refusal for the reference-layer/probe analysis)
Benign prompt set sourced from Winninger et al. (used for D_benign in the exploratory study)
quantitative_results: Normalized AUC of ASR-budget curve: TriageFuzz highest across all 6 target models, exceeding best baseline by >0.20 (20 points)
Gemma-7B: ASR=0.9 reached at budget 18 (TriageFuzz) vs 62 (best baseline), a 71.0% query reduction
Table 2 commercial API ASR@10/25 (TriageFuzz): GPT-3.5-Turbo 68.0%/94.0%; GPT-4o 50.0%/84.0%; Claude-3.5-Sonnet 47.5%/80.5%
Ablation (Uniform vs TriageFuzz) ASR gain at budgets 10/15/20/25: Gemma2-9B +21/+23/+27/+29; LLaMA3-8B +8/+21/+30/+32; Qwen2.5-7B +21/+19/+20/+18 points
Surrogate-model swap: ASR@25 varies by only 2.0-3.0 percentage points across the 6 target models
Attack-model swap: ASR@25 varies by 2.0-4.5 percentage points across the 6 target models
PPL-filter defense: <3 percentage-point ASR reduction versus undefended

SmoothLLM defense (Table 3, average ASR over budgets 10-25 before/after): Gemma-7B 85.6%->73.3%; Gemma2-9B 76.8%->57.4%; LLaMA3-8B 68.3%->53.5%; LLaMA3.2-3B 69.9%->61.1%; Qwen2.5-7B 87.9%->62.4%; Qwen2.5-3B 89.4%->70.1%
Cross-model Spearman correlation of refusal-tendency scores: first layer avg ~0.60 (high variance), reference layer l* mostly 0.86-0.98 in Fig.2b heatmap, final layer avg ~0.85
Cross-model cosine similarity of refusal semantic signature u1: mostly 0.80-0.98 across 6 model pairs (Fig. 3)
baselines_compared: Uniform (ablation: TriageFuzz with token-guidance disabled, random mutation positions)
